question: tell me about swing directions
content:
The direction of the pushaway is one of the most critical (and most underestimated) elements of the bowling swing.
The pushaway is the first programmed movement of the shot. It establishes the swing plane, determines how gravity will act on the ball, and dictates whether the swing can remain free and repeatable.
Because the bowling swing is a gravity driven pendulum, the direction in which the ball is sent during the pushaway directly controls everything that follows.
What Happens When the Pushaway Goes to the Side (The Wrong Way)
If, instead of moving the ball forward toward the target line, the bowler pushes the ball even slightly to the side of the body, several biomechanical problems appear almost immediately:
1. The ball moves behind the body at the top of the backswing
A sideways pushaway shifts the swing plane outward, forcing the ball to travel behind the bowler’s center of mass. Once the ball is behind the body, the swing is no longer vertical or aligned, it is displaced.
2. The swing path becomes round and circular
Instead of moving straight back and forward like a pendulum, the swing begins to wrap around the body. This circular motion reduces repeatability and makes it far harder to deliver the ball on the same launch angle shot after shot.
3. Accuracy and targeting suffer
A rounded swing cannot return to the same release point consistently. Because the swing must “find its way back” to the line, aiming becomes unreliable and directional misses increase.
4. Excessive or unstable rotation appears at release
As the swing moves around the body, the hand is forced to come around the side of the ball too early. This often creates over spin, unstable axis rotation, and an inconsistent roll. The motion starts to resemble a golf style swing, not a bowling pendulum.
For these reasons, a sideways pushaway is considered the wrong method: it introduces compensation, muscle use, and inconsistency, all enemies of control.

What’s the Right Way to Push the Ball Away
The correct pushaway is aligned with the body and directed toward the intended target line.
Key principles of a proper pushaway:
The ball moves forward, not sideways
The direction matches the target and breakpoint (E.T.T, Everything Towards the Target)
The arm stays relaxed, no steering or forcing
In modern bowling styles, the pushaway may be sent very slightly inside the visual target. This is intentional and controlled, not accidental.
This inside line helps keep the ball outside the body at the top of the backswing, allowing gravity to maintain a clean, vertical swing plane.
So, then why This Works?
When the pushaway is properly aligned:
1. The swing stays on plane
Gravity keeps the ball moving straight back and forward, not around the body. The ball naturally returns close to the ankle without effort.
2. Muscle use is minimized
Because the swing is aligned from the start, there is no need to pull, steer, or save the shot. The swing remains free and repeatable.
3. Release position improves automatically
With the swing returning under the shoulder and near the ankle, the hand stays under the ball longer. This produces cleaner roll, better leverage, and more controllable rotation.
4. Timing and balance improve
A straight pushaway allows the swing to sync naturally with the feet, making it far easier to arrive balanced at the foul line without rushing or grabbing.
And finally, why This Matters?
Alignment & Direction:
A straight pushaway keeps the entire motion aligned with the target, improving accuracy immediately.
Swing Shape:
Straight pushaway → straight pendulum → repeatable motion
Sideways pushaway → rounded swing → inconsistency
Release Quality:
Proper alignment allows the hand to stay under the ball, producing controlled power instead of forced spin.
